basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: The sūra of the Ants unfolds a fresco of prophetic knowledge and gratitude. After the opening letters Ṭā-Sīn and the praise of the clear Book, it evokes Moses before the fire on the mount — where the Name unveils itself and fear dissolves into nearness. Then come David and Solomon “to whom God gave knowledge”: Solomon, taught the speech of the birds, passes the valley of the ants where one of them warns her people; at her words the king smiles and gives thanks — and Qushayrī makes of that smile the confession of approval, and of that gratitude the very tether that holds the graces, for through thanksgiving they remain. The hoopoe brings news of Sheba, of Queen Bilqīs and her sun-worshipping people; the noble letter, opened by the basmala, seizes the queen's heart; her mighty throne is fetched “in the blink of an eye” by the one “who had knowledge from the Book,” and Solomon cries out: “This is from the favor of my Lord, to test me: shall I be thankful or ungrateful?” Bilqīs, tested by the disguised throne and then the crystal pavement, submits to God. There follow Ṣāliḥ and Thamūd, Lot and his people, then the great litany “Is there a god alongside God?” which culminates in the verse of the distressed: amman yujību l-muḍṭarra — “Is He not the One who answers the distressed when he calls upon Him and removes the evil?”, of which Qushayrī says that the call of the truly distressed, who sees in himself no recourse, has no veil. The sūra ends on the signs of the Hour and the pressing call to return before the time flees.
themes: the knowledge given to the prophets
the gratitude that holds the graces
Solomon and the Queen of Sheba
the call of the distressed answered
the signs of the Hour and the return

versets.ar: طس ۚ تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْقُرْآنِ وَكِتَابٍ مُبِينٍ
versets.tr: ṭā-sīn tilka āyātu l-qurʾāni wa-kitābin mubīnin
versets.trad: Ṭā. Sīn. These are the verses of the Qurʾān, of a clear Book;
versets.commentaire: Upon these isolated letters Qushayrī lets a hidden savor resound: by the purity of My holiness and the splendor of My glory, I do not disappoint the hope of one who hopes for My gentleness. The verses, he says, are the tokens of Our generosity, the marks of Our favor, the witnesses of Our kindness: through them God shows His friends the truth of His promise and the keeping of His covenant. The “clear Book” (mubīn) gives light to whoever turns his gaze toward it.

mots.racine: أ ي ي / ق ر أ
ك ت ب / ب ي ن

versets.ar: هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: hudan wa-bushrā li-l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: a guidance and good tidings for the believers,
versets.commentaire: These verses and this Book are, according to Qushayrī, exposition and healing, light and radiance, glad tidings and proof — but reserved for those for whom God has realized faith, confirmed the guarantee, and assured beneficence. Guidance precedes good tidings: one is first led, then gladdened.

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلَاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكَاةَ وَهُم بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna yuqīmūna l-ṣalāta wa-yuʾtūna l-zakāta wa-hum bi-l-ākhirati hum yūqinūna
versets.trad: those who establish the prayer, give the alms, and are certain of the Hereafter.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī raises the sense: they maintain the bonds with God and stand upright in the courtesies of intimate discourse. Their alms are not only of their wealth, but of their states, their motions and their stillnesses: they fulfill the rights of the believers as best they can and supply the place of the weak among them. Prayer and alms are crowned by certainty (yaqīn) of the Hereafter.

mots.racine: ق و م / ص ل و
أ ت ي / ز ك و

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna lā yuʾminūna bi-l-ākhirati zayyannā lahum aʿmālahum fa-hum yaʿmahūna
versets.trad: As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have adorned their deeds in their own eyes, and so they wander blindly.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sharpens the image: We have covered them with a veil, so that they no longer see; We have darkened the paths for them, so that they turn away from the best way. Those wander in their error and stumble in their bewilderment. The worst chastisement is that the fault should appear beautiful to the one who commits it.

versets.ar: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ وَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ
versets.tr: ulāʾika lladhīna lahum sūʾu l-ʿadhābi wa-hum fī l-ākhirati humu l-akhsarūna
versets.trad: It is they for whom is the worst chastisement, and in the Hereafter they are the greatest losers.
versets.commentaire: The “worst chastisement” (sūʾ al-ʿadhāb), says Qushayrī, is to find the pains without finding the consolation of knowing the One who consoles, to bear the trial without the vision of the One who sends it lightening its weight. For the believers, on the contrary, the chastisement is lightened by their fair hope in God, their supplication, then the divine grace of relief.

mots.racine: س و أ / ع ذ ب

versets.ar: وَإِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى الْقُرْآنَ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ
versets.tr: wa-innaka la-tulaqqā l-qurʾāna min ladun ḥakīmin ʿalīmin
versets.trad: And truly you receive the Qurʾān from One Wise, All-Knowing.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes of this verse a promise of protection: the One who honored you by sending down upon you the Qurʾān is also the One who guards you from evils, enemies, and kinds of trial. The gift of the Word is accompanied by the keeping of the messenger: the same Wisdom that reveals preserves.

mots.racine: ح ك م / ع ل م

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَهْلِهِ إِنِّي آنَسْتُ نَارًا سَآتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ آتِيكُم بِشِهَابٍ قَبَسٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla mūsā li-ahlihi innī ānastu nāran sa-ātīkum minhā bi-khabarin aw ātīkum bi-shihābin qabasin laʿallakum taṣṭalūna
versets.trad: When Moses said to his family: “Truly I have perceived a fire. I shall bring you news from it, or bring you a burning brand that you may warm yourselves.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī paints Moses' distress as he journeyed from Madyan toward Egypt: night enveloped him, his wife was seized by labor-pains, cold winds blew, the flint struck no spark; the matter closed in upon him, the moment grew dark, his resolve scattered, and preoccupation seized his heart. It was in this destitution that he glimpsed a fire afar. When a man is inhabited by reflection, in every thing he finds a lesson.

mots.racine: أ ن س / ن و ر
ش ه ب / ق ب س

mots.def: iṣṭalā: to warm at a fire.

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا جَاءَهَا نُودِيَ أَن بُورِكَ مَن فِي النَّارِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā jāʾahā nūdiya an būrika man fī l-nāri wa-man ḥawlahā wa-subḥāna llāhi rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: When he came to it, a voice called out: “Blessed is he who is in the fire and he who is around it! And glory be to God, Lord of the worlds!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī insists that what Moses heard was the Word of God itself, not the speech of the tree, against those who erred: had the call been in the tree, the tree would have been the speaker. “Blessed” (būrika) means: blessing has reached and overtaken him — and baraka is increase and growth in the good. That this benediction comes from the Eternal makes it real and brings it to pass.

versets.ar: يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنَّهُ أَنَا اللَّهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ
versets.tr: yā mūsā innahu anā llāhu l-ʿazīzu l-ḥakīmu
versets.trad: O Moses! It is I, God, the Mighty, the Wise.
versets.commentaire: He who addresses you, says Qushayrī in the name of the Real: “I am God, the Mighty” in the grandeur of My majesty, “the Wise” in all My acts. The Name unveils itself to Moses in the heart of the night, joining all-power to the wisdom that orders every thing.

versets.ar: وَأَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَآهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَانٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَا مُوسَىٰ لَا تَخَفْ إِنِّي لَا يَخَافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-alqi ʿaṣāka fa-lammā raʾāhā tahtazzu kaʾannahā jānnun wallā mudbiran wa-lam yuʿaqqib yā mūsā lā takhaf innī lā yakhāfu ladayya l-mursalūna
versets.trad: And cast down your staff!” But when he saw it writhing as if it were a serpent, he turned his back and did not return. “O Moses! Do not fear! With Me the messengers have no cause to fear,
versets.commentaire: God willed to show Moses, says Qushayrī, a sign vast enough to grant him the fullness of certainty. Moses' fear came from his very knowledge: he knew that God chastises whom He wills as He wills. So the Real reassures him: “Do not fear! With Me, the messengers have no cause to fear” — it does not befit them to fear. The nearness of the Lord dissolves the fear of His power.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / ع ص و
ه ز ز / ج ن ن

versets.ar: إِلَّا مَن ظَلَمَ ثُمَّ بَدَّلَ حُسْنًا بَعْدَ سُوءٍ فَإِنِّي غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
versets.tr: illā man ẓalama thumma baddala ḥusnan baʿda sūʾin fa-innī ghafūrun raḥīmun
versets.trad: save him who has wronged, then exchanged good for evil — for truly I am Forgiving, Merciful.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sees here the opening of forgiveness: whoever has committed a wrong then exchanges good after evil, on condition of not persisting in the fault, finds God forgiving and merciful. When Moses saw the staff transformed, he knew it was indeed the Real unveiling Himself to him through this sign. The repentance that substitutes good for evil effaces what came before.

mots.racine: ب د ل / ح س ن
غ ف ر / ر ح م

versets.ar: وَأَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ ۖ فِي تِسْعِ آيَاتٍ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَقَوْمِهِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا قَوْمًا فَاسِقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-adkhil yadaka fī jaybika takhruj bayḍāʾa min ghayri sūʾin fī tisʿi āyātin ilā firʿawna wa-qawmihi innahum kānū qawman fāsiqīna
versets.trad: And put your hand into your bosom; it will come out white without blemish. This is among nine signs to Pharaoh and his people. They are indeed a depraved people.”
versets.commentaire: “Without blemish,” Qushayrī clarifies, means without leprosy: the whiteness is light, not disease. And the Real consoles Moses for his nightly distress: “We have spared you that worry, We have taken charge of your wife and your affairs, We have gathered your sheep and your oxen, and your wife is restored to you safe.” God provides for the servant's cares before he even confides them.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ج ي ب
mots.sens: put your hand into your bosom

mots.racine: ت س ع / أ ي ي
mots.def: the nine miracles given to Moses.

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا جَاءَتْهُمْ آيَاتُنَا مُبْصِرَةً قَالُوا هَٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: fa-lammā jāʾathum āyātunā mubṣiratan qālū hādhā siḥrun mubīnun
versets.trad: But when Our signs came to them, giving sight, they said: “This is manifest sorcery!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī affirms that God never manifested a sign through a prophet without its being so clear that, had they directed their gaze upon it rightly, they would have reached knowledge and the stilling of the heart. But some neglected to look, and others recognized the sign yet repelled it through denial. The sign is luminous: it is the closed eye that makes the darkness.

mots.racine: س ح ر / ب ي ن

versets.ar: وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا ۚ فَانظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ
versets.tr: wa-jaḥadū bihā wa-stayqanathā anfusuhum ẓulman wa-ʿuluwwan fa-nẓur kayfa kāna ʿāqibatu l-mufsidīna
versets.trad: They denied them, though their souls were convinced of them, out of injustice and arrogance. So see how was the end of the sowers of corruption!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī likens this denial to the worst of sins: as the disbeliever denies what his soul inwardly knows for certain, so the sinner knows with certainty that his act is unlawful; the deterring thoughts crowd upon his heart without his forgetting them, yet he goes ahead anyway out of indulgence to his passion. And this kind of sin — committed in full knowledge — is the most ill-fated, the most heavily chastised, the farthest from forgiveness.

mots.def: jaḥada: to deny against the evidence.

mots.racine: ي ق ن / ن ف س
mots.sens: their souls were convinced of them

mots.racine: ظ ل م / ع ل و

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ عِلْمًا ۖ وَقَالَا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي فَضَّلَنَا عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّنْ عِبَادِهِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ātaynā dāwūda wa-sulaymāna ʿilman wa-qālā l-ḥamdu li-llāhi lladhī faḍḍalanā ʿalā kathīrin min ʿibādihi l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: And We gave David and Solomon knowledge, and they said: “Praise be to God who has favored us over many of His believing servants!”
versets.commentaire: This verse, says Qushayrī, indicates that God singled them out with a kind of knowledge that no other shared with them. And he finds in it the proof that the excellence acquired through knowledge is acquired by no other attribute: so they gave thanks. The learned are the heirs of the prophets; knowledge is a blessing that demands gratitude. The learned man must believe that, if he was preferred over many, many too were preferred over him.

versets.ar: وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُودَ ۖ وَقَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ الطَّيْرِ وَأُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ ۖ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَهُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْمُبِينُ
versets.tr: wa-waritha sulaymānu dāwūda wa-qāla yā ayyuhā l-nāsu ʿullimnā manṭiqa l-ṭayri wa-ūtīnā min kulli shayʾin inna hādhā la-huwa l-faḍlu l-mubīnu
versets.trad: And Solomon inherited David, and he said: “O people! We have been taught the speech of the birds, and we have been given of all things. This is indeed the manifest favor.”
versets.commentaire: Solomon inherited from his father prophethood and rank. For Qushayrī, understanding the speech of the birds was a manifest sign, but he draws from it a spiritual law: whoever possesses clear-sightedness and the presence of the heart with God contemplates all things through God and from God; he is unveiled to them on the side of understanding, as if he heard from each thing the endless teachings the Real addresses to the servant. For the present heart, everything becomes speech.

mots.racine: ن ط ق / ط ي ر
ف ض ل / ب ي ن

versets.ar: وَحُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمَانَ جُنُودُهُ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ وَالطَّيْرِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-ḥushira li-sulaymāna junūduhu mina l-jinni wa-l-insi wa-l-ṭayri fa-hum yūzaʿūna
versets.trad: And gathered for Solomon were his armies of jinn, men, and birds, and they were marshaled in ranks.
versets.commentaire: God subjected to Solomon the jinn and the birds, says Qushayrī: the jinn were bound by the obligations of the Law, and the birds were subdued while having a share of Law; likewise the animals of his time, even the ants, whose discourse Solomon understood and over whom his judgment was carried out. The reign of the prophet sets in order the visible and invisible worlds.

versets.ar: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَتَوْا عَلَىٰ وَادِ النَّمْلِ قَالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّمْلُ ادْخُلُوا مَسَاكِنَكُمْ لَا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمَانُ وَجُنُودُهُ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ
versets.tr: ḥattā idhā ataw ʿalā wādi l-namli qālat namlatun yā ayyuhā l-namlu dkhulū masākinakum lā yaḥṭimannakum sulaymānu wa-junūduhu wa-hum lā yashʿurūna
versets.trad: Until, when they came upon the valley of the ants, an ant said: “O ants! Enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his armies crush you without being aware.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports a startling saying ascribed to the ant: “I urge my people to renounce this lower world; I feared that, seeing you in your sovereignty, they would covet it, so I ordered them to enter, lest their asceticism be disturbed.” And he concludes: this verse shows the beauty of taking precaution against what is feared, which the very nature of the soul and the discernment in which it was created dictate.

mots.racine: و د ي / ن م ل
د خ ل / س ك ن

versets.ar: فَتَبَسَّمَ ضَاحِكًا مِّن قَوْلِهَا وَقَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَالِدَيَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَالِحًا تَرْضَاهُ وَأَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبَادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ
versets.tr: fa-tabassama ḍāḥikan min qawlihā wa-qāla rabbi awziʿnī an ashkura niʿmataka llatī anʿamta ʿalayya wa-ʿalā wālidayya wa-an aʿmala ṣāliḥan tarḍāhu wa-adkhilnī bi-raḥmatika fī ʿibādika l-ṣāliḥīna
versets.trad: So he smiled, laughing at her words, and said: “My Lord! Inspire me to be thankful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents, and to do good that pleases You, and admit me by Your mercy among Your righteous servants!”
versets.commentaire: For Qushayrī, the smile is rare among kings, mindful of the rules of rule; it bears witness, then, to Solomon's approval of the ant's wise conduct toward her people. And his request reveals that his gaze upon creation was a gaze of meditation: he saw that the very teaching God gave him was among the blessings that demand gratitude. That he says “and upon my parents” shows that the servant's thankfulness is not confined to what is his alone.

mots.racine: ب س م / ض ح ك
و ز ع / ش ك ر
د خ ل / ر ح م

versets.ar: وَتَفَقَّدَ الطَّيْرَ فَقَالَ مَا لِيَ لَا أَرَى الْهُدْهُدَ أَمْ كَانَ مِنَ الْغَائِبِينَ
versets.tr: wa-tafaqqada l-ṭayra fa-qāla mā liya lā arā l-hudhuda am kāna mina l-ghāʾibīna
versets.trad: And he reviewed the birds and said: “How is it that I do not see the hoopoe? Or is it among the absent?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī admires Solomon's vigilance here: he guarded his kingdom and watched excellently over the affairs of his people, so that the absence of one bird among the smallest, missing for but a single hour, did not escape him. That the shepherd of souls should notice the humblest straying sheep: such is the perfection of pastoral care.

mots.racine: ف ق د / ط ي ر

mots.sens: how is it I do not see

versets.ar: لَأُعَذِّبَنَّهُ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا أَوْ لَأَذْبَحَنَّهُ أَوْ لَيَأْتِيَنِّي بِسُلْطَانٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: la-uʿadhdhibannahu ʿadhāban shadīdan aw la-adhbaḥannahu aw la-yaʾtiyannī bi-sulṭānin mubīnin
versets.trad: I shall surely punish it with a severe punishment, or slaughter it, unless it brings me a clear authority.”
versets.commentaire: This verse shows, says Qushayrī, that the weight of the fault is not measured by the smallness or greatness of the body. And he transposes this “severe punishment” onto the spiritual path, as in his Risāla: the severest punishment is to be stripped of contentment and tried by greed; that the servant be deprived of the sweetness of service and feel its pain; that he be abandoned to his own strength; the avidity in the quest followed by being barred from attainment.

mots.racine: س ل ط / ب ي ن

versets.ar: فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ فَقَالَ أَحَطتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ
versets.tr: fa-makatha ghayra baʿīdin fa-qāla aḥaṭtu bi-mā lam tuḥiṭ bihi wa-jiʾtuka min sabaʾin bi-nabaʾin yaqīnin
versets.trad: It stayed not long, then said: “I have grasped what you have not grasped, and I bring you sure news from Sheba.
versets.commentaire: The hoopoe returned, knowing Solomon had threatened it, and said: “I have encompassed in knowledge what escaped you, and I bring you sure news from Sheba.” Qushayrī notes the delicacy: the small creature, through its truthfulness, will become worthy of an embassy. True knowledge raises the humblest to the rank of envoy.

mots.racine: م ك ث / ب ع د
ن ب أ / ي ق ن

versets.ar: إِنِّي وَجَدتُّ امْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَأُوتِيَتْ مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ
versets.tr: innī wajadtu mraʾatan tamlikuhum wa-ūtiyat min kulli shayʾin wa-lahā ʿarshun ʿaẓīmun
versets.trad: Indeed I found a woman ruling over them, given of all things, and she has a mighty throne.
versets.commentaire: The hoopoe reports the reign of Bilqīs, her wealth, her power, and her mighty throne. Qushayrī stresses what will follow: Solomon was not stirred by this, and greed did not seize him at what he heard — contrary to the habit of kings who covet the kingdom of others. Greatness received from God renders the heart deaf to covetousness.

mots.racine: و ج د / م ر أ
ع ر ش / ع ظ م

versets.ar: وَجَدتُّهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: wajadtuhā wa-qawmahā yasjudūna li-l-shamsi min dūni llāhi wa-zayyana lahumu l-shayṭānu aʿmālahum fa-ṣaddahum ʿani l-sabīli fa-hum lā yahtadūna
versets.trad: I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of God; Satan has adorned their deeds for them and barred them from the way, so they are not guided.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports that it was at these words — the worship of the sun instead of God — that Solomon was seized with anger for God's sake. Satan “adorns” the deeds: it is the same veil cast over the disbelievers at the sūra's opening. When the evil act appears beautiful, error becomes a road, and guidance withdraws.

mots.racine: س ج د / ش م س

versets.ar: أَلَّا يَسْجُدُوا لِلَّهِ الَّذِي يُخْرِجُ الْخَبْءَ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُخْفُونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ
versets.tr: allā yasjudū li-llāhi lladhī yukhriju l-khabʾa fī l-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-yaʿlamu mā tukhfūna wa-mā tuʿlinūna
versets.trad: Why do they not prostrate to God, who brings forth what is hidden in the heavens and the earth, and knows what you conceal and what you reveal!
themes: the God who unveils the hidden
versets.commentaire: The hoopoe — or the wisdom that speaks through it — redirects worship to the One who brings forth the hidden (al-khabʾ) of the heavens and the earth and who knows the secret and the declared. Qushayrī makes of this winged creature a herald of oneness: whoever sees God behind every thing can prostrate only to Him, the source of all that is unveiled.

mots.racine: خ ر ج / خ ب أ

versets.ar: اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ ۩
versets.tr: allāhu lā ilāha illā huwa rabbu l-ʿarshi l-ʿaẓīmi
versets.trad: God — there is no god but He, Lord of the mighty Throne!”
versets.commentaire: To the “mighty throne” of Bilqīs (v. 23) responds here the “mighty Throne” of God: every earthly kingdom fades before the Lordship of the Real. Qushayrī makes of this verse the apex of the hoopoe's call: there is none worshipped but He, and no greatness subsists before His. It is the summit of tawḥīd at the heart of the story.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ر ش / ع ظ م

versets.ar: قَالَ سَنَنظُرُ أَصَدَقْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ الْكَاذِبِينَ
versets.tr: qāla sananẓuru a-ṣadaqta am kunta mina l-kādhibīna
versets.trad: He said: “We shall see whether you have told the truth or are among the liars.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws from this verse a rule of discernment: the report of a single witness does not produce certain knowledge; one must pause over it as something possible, without rejecting it, but verifying: is it true or false? And when Solomon learned the excuse, he abandoned his threat — such is the way of the just ruler: his justice keeps him from oppressing his subjects, and he accepts the excuse of one found in the posture of the guilty if he is sincere in his plea.

versets.ar: اذْهَب بِّكِتَابِي هَٰذَا فَأَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَانظُرْ مَاذَا يَرْجِعُونَ
versets.tr: idhhab bi-kitābī hādhā fa-alqih ilayhim thumma tawalla ʿanhum fa-nẓur mādhā yarjiʿūna
versets.trad: Go with this letter of mine and deliver it to them; then withdraw from them and see what they return.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sees a subtle indication: it does not befit one to utter every word before kings, for he draws trouble upon himself thereby. Solomon had multitudes of servants, yet he charged with this mission only the hoopoe, because it was the one who had spoken: it therefore fell to it to answer for what it had said. And since it was truthful, it was raised — despite the weakness of its form — to the dignity of an ambassador.

mots.racine: ذ ه ب / ك ت ب

versets.ar: قَالَتْ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ إِنِّي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيَّ كِتَابٌ كَرِيمٌ
versets.tr: qālat yā ayyuhā l-malaʾu innī ulqiya ilayya kitābun karīmun
versets.trad: She said: “O notables! A noble letter has been cast to me.
versets.commentaire: “A noble letter” (kitāb karīm): Qushayrī recalls several senses of this nobility — it set aside all baseness; it was sealed — for “the nobility of a letter is its seal”; it was borne by a bird, a sign that its sender was of immense rank; it began with “In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.” The letter seized her heart and overpowered her, so that she acknowledged its worth — and through this regard attained the safeguarding of her kingdom and the gift of Islam.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ك ر م

versets.ar: إِنَّهُ مِن سُلَيْمَانَ وَإِنَّهُ بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ
versets.tr: innahu min sulaymāna wa-innahu bismi llāhi l-raḥmāni l-raḥīmi
versets.trad: It is from Solomon, and it is: ‘In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes the basmala the very mark of the letter's nobility, and from it draws an analogy for prayer: if the letter is noble through the formula of the Name, the noble prayer is the one that does not divest itself of the mention of the Name — and if it divests itself of it, the matter is reversed. The letter opens like a prayer: by the Name of the All-Merciful.

mots.racine: س م و / أ ل ه

versets.ar: أَلَّا تَعْلُوا عَلَيَّ وَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ
versets.tr: allā taʿlū ʿalayya wa-ʾtūnī muslimīna
versets.trad: do not exalt yourselves against me, but come to me in submission.’”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī insists: the letter contained no covetousness of the kingdom, but a call to God: “Do not exalt yourselves against me, and come to me submitting.” Solomon demands neither tribute nor land, but surrender to God. Islam here is not conquest but the inclining of the heart toward the One.

mots.sens: do not exalt yourselves against me
mots.def: ʿalā: to be haughty, rise up.

versets.ar: قَالَتْ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِي فِي أَمْرِي مَا كُنتُ قَاطِعَةً أَمْرًا حَتَّىٰ تَشْهَدُونِ
versets.tr: qālat yā ayyuhā l-malaʾu aftūnī fī amrī mā kuntu qāṭiʿatan amran ḥattā tashhadūni
versets.trad: She said: “O notables! Advise me in my affair; I never decide a matter unless you are present with me.”
versets.commentaire: Bilqīs took counsel, as great affairs require. Qushayrī makes of it a lesson in governance: the sovereign must not shut himself within his own opinion alone; he needs a circle of men of judgment and clear sight. The pagan queen gives here a lesson in prudence that many a king ignores.

versets.ar: قَالُوا نَحْنُ أُولُو قُوَّةٍ وَأُولُو بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ وَالْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ فَانظُرِي مَاذَا تَأْمُرِينَ
versets.tr: qālū naḥnu ulū quwwatin wa-ulū baʾsin shadīdin wa-l-amru ilayki fa-nẓurī mādhā taʾmurīna
versets.trad: They said: “We are men of strength and great valor, but the command is yours; so consider what you will command.”
versets.commentaire: The notables answered, says Qushayrī, according to courtesy (adab): “It rests with us only to exert every effort and to manifest our sincere counsel; but to follow the order and carry it out — that is yours.” They offer their strength while leaving the decision: obedience that counsels without usurping.

mots.racine: ب أ س / ش د د

versets.ar: قَالَتْ إِنَّ الْمُلُوكَ إِذَا دَخَلُوا قَرْيَةً أَفْسَدُوهَا وَجَعَلُوا أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِهَا أَذِلَّةً ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ
versets.tr: qālat inna l-mulūka idhā dakhalū qaryatan afsadūhā wa-jaʿalū aʿizzata ahlihā adhillatan wa-kadhālika yafʿalūna
versets.trad: She said: “When kings enter a town, they ruin it and make the noblest of its people the lowliest. Thus do they act.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī broadens the queen's words into an allegory of the heart: the noble virtues, when they touch the servant's heart, drive out the passions and base inclinations — rancor, envy, avarice, smallness — and establish the lofty states. But when the rabble of the soul and the blameworthy traits seize the heart, they ruin its prosperity: then the dwellings of the realities collapse and the houses of praiseworthy qualities sink at sunset. The sacked town is the image of the soul handed over to its low instincts.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ق ر ي
ع ز ز / ذ ل ل

versets.ar: وَإِنِّي مُرْسِلَةٌ إِلَيْهِم بِهَدِيَّةٍ فَنَاظِرَةٌ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ الْمُرْسَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-innī mursilatun ilayhim bi-hadiyyatin fa-nāẓiratun bi-ma yarjiʿu l-mursalūna
versets.trad: And I shall send them a gift, then see with what the envoys return.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recounts the story: the queen sent gifts — bricks of gold and silver. God informed Solomon of it, who had a courtyard paved with gold and silver and left two empty spaces at the entrance. When the envoys saw this ground, what they carried became small in their eyes; they were ashamed to offer it. The heart turned toward God is not seduced by the gold that worldly men think precious.

mots.racine: ر ج ع / ر س ل

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا جَاءَ سُلَيْمَانَ قَالَ أَتُمِدُّونَنِ بِمَالٍ فَمَا آتَانِيَ اللَّهُ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا آتَاكُم بَلْ أَنتُم بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā jāʾa sulaymāna qāla a-tumiddūnani bi-mālin fa-mā ātāniya llāhu khayrun mimmā ātākum bal antum bi-hadiyyatikum tafraḥūna
versets.trad: When it came to Solomon, he said: “Do you offer me wealth? What God has given me is better than what He has given you. Rather, it is you who rejoice in your gift!
versets.commentaire: Solomon refuses the gift: “Do you offer me wealth? Is one like me won over by such acts?” Qushayrī explains: you are a people who know only the surface of this lower world, so you rejoice in what increases for you; but my state is the opposite of yours: through what God has given me, I am free of the portions of this world. The prophet's detachment humbles the gold of kings.

mots.racine: م د د / م و ل
mots.sens: do you aid me with wealth

mots.racine: خ ي ر / أ ت ي
mots.sens: better than what He gave you

versets.ar: ارْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم بِجُنُودٍ لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بِهَا وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم مِّنْهَا أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ
versets.tr: irjiʿ ilayhim fa-la-naʾtiyannahum bi-junūdin lā qibala lahum bihā wa-la-nukhrijannahum minhā adhillatan wa-hum ṣāghirūna
versets.trad: Return to them; we shall surely come to them with armies they cannot face, and we shall expel them from it, humbled and brought low.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī continues with the envoys' return: when they came back to Bilqīs and reported what they had seen and heard, she knew there was no recourse but surrender and obedience, and resolved to go to his service. Solomon's threat is not covetousness but a pressing call: the firmness that leads to the threshold of submission.

mots.racine: ذ ل ل / ص غ ر

versets.ar: قَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَيُّكُمْ يَأْتِينِي بِعَرْشِهَا قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ
versets.tr: qāla yā ayyuhā l-malaʾu ayyukum yaʾtīnī bi-ʿarshihā qabla an yaʾtūnī muslimīna
versets.trad: He said: “O notables! Which of you will bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?”
versets.commentaire: God had expanded Solomon's reign, says Qushayrī: he held under his authority the jinn by subjection, men by obedience, and the demons in several categories. Solomon issues the challenge: who will bring the throne before the submitting ones arrive? The miracle is being prepared at the very heart of the assembly.

mots.sens: which of you will bring me

versets.ar: قَالَ عِفْرِيتٌ مِّنَ الْجِنِّ أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَن تَقُومَ مِن مَّقَامِكَ وَإِنِّي عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِيٌّ أَمِينٌ
versets.tr: qāla ʿifrītun mina l-jinni anā ātīka bihi qabla an taqūma min maqāmika wa-innī ʿalayhi la-qawiyyun amīnun
versets.trad: An ʿifrīt of the jinn said: “I will bring it to you before you rise from your place; for I am strong and trustworthy for it.”
versets.commentaire: The ʿifrīt, the strongest of the jinn, offers to bring the throne before Solomon rises. But, notes Qushayrī, Solomon showed no desire for his words, because he had founded them on the claim of his own strength. He sees in this a malāmatī regard: the refusal of every claim of the soul and every display of self. Strength that vaunts itself does not draw the prophet's gaze.

mots.racine: ع ف ر / ج ن ن
ق و ي / أ م ن

versets.ar: قَالَ الَّذِي عِندَهُ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَن يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَآهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِندَهُ قَالَ هَٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ ۖ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ
versets.tr: qāla lladhī ʿindahu ʿilmun mina l-kitābi anā ātīka bihi qabla an yartadda ilayka ṭarfuka fa-lammā raʾāhu mustaqirran ʿindahu qāla hādhā min faḍli rabbī li-yabluwanī a-ashkuru am akfuru wa-man shakara fa-innamā yashkuru li-nafsihi wa-man kafara fa-inna rabbī ghaniyyun karīmun
versets.trad: The one who had knowledge from the Book said: “I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you.” Then when he saw it set before him, he said: “This is from the favor of my Lord, to test me whether I am thankful or ungrateful. Whoever is thankful is thankful only for his own good; and whoever is ungrateful — my Lord is Self-Sufficient, Generous.”
versets.commentaire: “The one who had knowledge from the Book” was, it is said, Āṣaf, a man of charisma (karāma). Qushayrī recalls that the charisms of the saints join the miracles of the prophets, and that bringing the throne in the blink of an eye belongs only to divine power. Above all he meditates on Solomon's response: before the prodigy, he set about giving thanks, acknowledging the immensity of the blessing, full of modesty and humility: “This is from the favor of my Lord” — not by merit of mine nor by power of another. And he defines gratitude: to praise the benefactor by recalling his benefaction; and, on the path of the spiritual, to spend the blessing in service. Gratitude is the tether that holds the graces, for through it they remain.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ك ت ب
ر د د / ط ر ف
ف ض ل / ر ب ب
mots.sens: this is from my Lord's favor

mots.racine: غ ن ي / ك ر م

versets.ar: قَالَ نَكِّرُوا لَهَا عَرْشَهَا نَنظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِي أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: qāla nakkirū lahā ʿarshahā nanẓur a-tahtadī am takūnu mina lladhīna lā yahtadūna
versets.trad: He said: “Disguise her throne for her; we shall see whether she is guided or is among those who are not guided.”
versets.commentaire: Solomon wished to test the queen's intelligence, says Qushayrī, and ordered her throne altered. The alteration of the throne — turned upside down — will become for her a sign: will she recognize, within the upheaval, what abides? The test does not humble but reveals the fineness of discernment.

mots.racine: ن ك ر / ع ر ش

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا جَاءَتْ قِيلَ أَهَٰكَذَا عَرْشُكِ ۖ قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُ هُوَ ۚ وَأُوتِينَا الْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā jāʾat qīla a-hākadhā ʿarshuki qālat kaʾannahu huwa wa-ūtīnā l-ʿilma min qablihā wa-kunnā muslimīna
versets.trad: When she came, it was said: “Is your throne like this?” She said: “It is as though it were it.” And we were given knowledge before her, and we were submitters.
versets.commentaire: “It is as though it were it”: by this measured reply — neither affirmation nor denial — the queen bears witness, says Qushayrī, to the perfection of her intelligence. To recognize her transformed throne was for her an event breaking the ordinary, hence a sign attesting the truth of Solomon's prophethood. The fineness of the mind prepares the surrender of faith.

versets.ar: وَصَدَّهَا مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَافِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-ṣaddahā mā kānat taʿbudu min dūni llāhi innahā kānat min qawmin kāfirīna
versets.trad: What she used to worship besides God had barred her. Indeed she was of a disbelieving people.
versets.commentaire: What she used to worship besides God — the sun — had turned her away. For Qushayrī, this verse seals her past: born of a disbelieving people, she is now wrenched from it. The false object of worship leads astray as long as it veils the One; the encounter with Solomon lifts that veil.

versets.ar: قِيلَ لَهَا ادْخُلِي الصَّرْحَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَكَشَفَتْ عَن سَاقَيْهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُ صَرْحٌ مُّمَرَّدٌ مِّن قَوَارِيرَ ۗ قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمَانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: qīla lahā dkhulī l-ṣarḥa fa-lammā raʾathu ḥasibathu lujjatan wa-kashafat ʿan sāqayhā qāla innahu ṣarḥun mumarradun min qawārīra qālat rabbi innī ẓalamtu nafsī wa-aslamtu maʿa sulaymāna li-llāhi rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: It was said to her: “Enter the palace.” But when she saw it she supposed it a pool of water and bared her legs. He said: “It is a palace paved smooth with crystal.” She said: “My Lord! I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to God, Lord of the worlds!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recounts the final test: Solomon had a floor of transparent glass set over a deep pool; seeing the water beneath the crystal, Bilqīs thought she must wade across and bared her legs. This was a cause and ground of her certainty. Then she pronounced the word of surrender: “My Lord, I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to God, Lord of the worlds.” The crystal palace, image of the deceiving world, becomes the threshold of faith.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ص ر ح
ح س ب / ل ج ج
م ر د / ق ر ر
ظ ل م / ن ف س

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَالِحًا أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ فَإِذَا هُمْ فَرِيقَانِ يَخْتَصِمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad arsalnā ilā thamūda akhāhum ṣāliḥan ani ʿbudū llāha fa-idhā hum farīqāni yakhtaṣimūna
versets.trad: And We sent to Thamūd their brother Ṣāliḥ, saying: “Worship God!” But behold, they split into two contending factions.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī here opens the account of Thamūd and their prophet Ṣāliḥ: the denial they opposed to him, their demand for a miracle, the story of the she-camel and its hamstringing. The call to worship the One at once divides the people into two camps: the Word of truth always separates those who receive it from those who reject it.

mots.racine: ف ر ق / خ ص م

versets.ar: قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ لِمَ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ الْحَسَنَةِ ۖ لَوْلَا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ
versets.tr: qāla yā qawmi lima tastaʿjilūna bi-l-sayyiʾati qabla l-ḥasanati lawlā tastaghfirūna llāha laʿallakum turḥamūna
versets.trad: He said: “O my people, why do you hasten to evil before good? If only you sought God's forgiveness, that you might be shown mercy.”
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ reproaches his people for rushing to evil before good and calls them to seek forgiveness. Qushayrī, faithful to his pedagogy, sees in the seeking of forgiveness (istighfār) the door of mercy: hastening to evil estranges, but asking pardon opens hope. The divine “perhaps” (laʿalla) is already a veiled promise.

mots.racine: س و أ / ح س ن

versets.ar: قَالُوا اطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَن مَّعَكَ ۚ قَالَ طَائِرُكُمْ عِندَ اللَّهِ ۖ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ
versets.tr: qālū ṭṭayyarnā bika wa-bi-man maʿaka qāla ṭāʾirukum ʿinda llāhi bal antum qawmun tuftanūna
versets.trad: They said: “We augur ill of you and of those with you.” He said: “Your augury is with God; rather, you are a people being tested.”
versets.commentaire: They accuse Ṣāliḥ of ill omen; he answers that their “augury” is with God — that is, all that befalls them belongs to the divine decree, not to any outward sign. Qushayrī reads in it the trial (fitna): they are tested so that what is within them may be revealed. The superstitious soul projects outward the cause of its own ill.

mots.def: taṭayyara: to see an evil omen.

versets.ar: وَكَانَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ
versets.tr: wa-kāna fī l-madīnati tisʿatu rahṭin yufsidūna fī l-arḍi wa-lā yuṣliḥūna
versets.trad: And there were in the city nine men who sowed corruption in the land and did not set things right.
versets.commentaire: Nine men spread corruption and never reformed. Qushayrī, following the thread of the narrative, retains the contrast between ifsād (to corrupt) and iṣlāḥ (to reform): the hardened heart sows only ruin. Those who refuse all amendment prepare their own destruction.

mots.racine: ت س ع / ر ه ط
mots.def: rahṭ: a small group of men.

versets.ar: قَالُوا تَقَاسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ لَنُبَيِّتَنَّهُ وَأَهْلَهُ ثُمَّ لَنَقُولَنَّ لِوَلِيِّهِ مَا شَهِدْنَا مَهْلِكَ أَهْلِهِ وَإِنَّا لَصَادِقُونَ
versets.tr: qālū taqāsamū bi-llāhi la-nubayyitannahu wa-ahlahu thumma la-naqūlanna li-waliyyihi mā shahidnā mahlika ahlihi wa-innā la-ṣādiqūna
versets.trad: They said: “Swear to one another by God: we shall attack him and his family by night, then say to his next of kin: ‘We did not witness the destruction of his family; indeed we are truthful.’”
versets.commentaire: The conspirators swear by God to kill Ṣāliḥ by night, then to deny it before the avenger of blood. Qushayrī underscores the tragic irony: they take an oath in the very name of the One they betray. The false oath adds lying to murder — they think they scheme, but their scheme closes upon them.

mots.sens: swear to one another by God
we shall attack him by night

mots.racine: ش ه د / ه ل ك
mots.sens: we did not witness his family's death

versets.ar: وَمَكَرُوا مَكْرًا وَمَكَرْنَا مَكْرًا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-makarū makran wa-makarnā makran wa-hum lā yashʿurūna
versets.trad: They devised a scheme, and We devised a scheme, while they were unaware.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes the two “schemes”: theirs was to feign agreement with Ṣāliḥ while hamstringing the she-camel in secret and casting the crime upon an innocent; God's scheme was to conceal from them the chastisement He had prepared, then to bring it down upon them suddenly. And he adds, fearsomely: among His subtle schemes is the renown of piety while one acts in secret to the contrary — but in the Hereafter this false coin no longer passes.

versets.ar: فَانظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ مَكْرِهِمْ أَنَّا دَمَّرْنَاهُمْ وَقَوْمَهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ
versets.tr: fa-nẓur kayfa kāna ʿāqibatu makrihim annā dammarnāhum wa-qawmahum ajmaʿīna
versets.trad: So see how was the outcome of their scheme: We destroyed them and their people, all together.
versets.commentaire: “See the end of their scheme”: God annihilated them all, sparing not one, says Qushayrī. Human scheming, however clever, has but one end when it rises against the decree: destruction. The contemplation of their end is itself an exhortation.

mots.racine: ع ق ب / م ك ر

versets.ar: فَتِلْكَ بُيُوتُهُمْ خَاوِيَةً بِمَا ظَلَمُوا ۗ إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَآيَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: fa-tilka buyūtuhum khāwiyatan bi-mā ẓalamū inna fī dhālika la-āyatan li-qawmin yaʿlamūna
versets.trad: So those are their houses, fallen empty because of their wrongdoing. In that is indeed a sign for a people who know.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī cites the ḥadīth: “If injustice were a house in Paradise, God would send ruin upon it.” And he transposes: souls, when they oppress themselves by their faults, fall to ruin under the weight of humiliation, until their owner grows used to sloth and is deprived of divine aid. The ruin of souls comes from the dominion of passion; that of hearts, from the dominion of heedlessness and hardness; that of spirits, from the dominion of the veil; that of secrets, from the dominion of absence and desolation.

mots.racine: ب ي ت / خ و ي
أ ي ي / ع ل م
mots.sens: a sign for those who know

versets.ar: وَأَنجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: wa-anjaynā lladhīna āmanū wa-kānū yattaqūna
versets.trad: And We saved those who believed and were godfearing.
versets.commentaire: At the heart of the destruction, God saves the godfearing believers. Qushayrī contrasts this salvation with the preceding ruin: faith and reverential awe (taqwā) build up the soul where injustice lays it waste. The very catastrophe that engulfs the unjust spares those whose hearts are turned toward God.

versets.ar: وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lūṭan idh qāla li-qawmihi a-taʾtūna l-fāḥishata wa-antum tubṣirūna
versets.trad: And Lot, when he said to his people: “Do you commit the abomination while you see?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī moves to the account of Lot: his people's obstinacy in abomination, the chastisement that fell, the loss of the complicit wife, the prophet's deliverance, and the affair of the angels sent to destroy them. “While you see”: their sin is not ignorance but willful blindness — they commit the infamy with open eyes.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ف ح ش

versets.ar: أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ النِّسَاءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ
versets.tr: a-innakum la-taʾtūna l-rijāla shahwatan min dūni l-nisāʾi bal antum qawmun tajhalūna
versets.trad: Do you approach men with desire instead of women? Indeed you are an ignorant people!”
versets.commentaire: Lot names the fault and calls it ignorance (jahl). For Qushayrī, in the line of the verse, this “ignorance” is not lack of learning but the blindness of passion: concupiscence turned from its natural order darkens discernment. Disordered desire is but a form of the jāhiliyya of the heart.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ر ج ل

versets.ar: فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَن قَالُوا أَخْرِجُوا آلَ لُوطٍ مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ
versets.tr: fa-mā kāna jawāba qawmihi illā an qālū akhrijū āla lūṭin min qaryatikum innahum unāsun yataṭahharūna
versets.trad: The only answer of his people was to say: “Expel the family of Lot from your town; they are people who would keep pure.”
versets.commentaire: The people's only answer was to banish Lot and his family, mocking their “purity.” Qushayrī notes the reversal: they make virtue a grievance. When the soul is corrupt, another's purity becomes unbearable to it: they expel the very thing that could save them.

mots.racine: خ ر ج / أ و ل

versets.ar: فَأَنجَيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ قَدَّرْنَاهَا مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ
versets.tr: fa-anjaynāhu wa-ahlahu illā mraʾatahu qaddarnāhā mina l-ghābirīna
versets.trad: So We saved him and his family, except his wife; We decreed her to be among those who lagged behind.
versets.commentaire: God saves Lot and his family, except his wife, decreed among those who remain in the chastisement. Qushayrī, in the line of the verse, sees in it the mystery of the decree (taqdīr): nearness to the prophet does not save the one whose heart has refused. The bond of flesh does not hold where the bond of faith is wanting.

mots.racine: ن ج و / أ ه ل
mots.sens: We saved him and his family

mots.racine: ق د ر / غ ب ر
mots.sens: We decreed her among those who lagged

versets.ar: وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَاءَ مَطَرُ الْمُنذَرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-amṭarnā ʿalayhim maṭaran fa-sāʾa maṭaru l-mundharīna
versets.trad: And We rained upon them a rain; and evil was the rain of those who had been warned.
versets.commentaire: Upon them fell a fatal rain — a rain of stones, the chastisement of the warned who took no heed of the warning. Qushayrī, following the verse, contrasts the rain that ordinarily gives life with the one here that brings death: what is mercy for some becomes chastisement for whoever has rejected the warning.

mots.racine: س و أ / م ط ر

versets.ar: قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ عِبَادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَىٰ ۗ آللَّهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
versets.tr: quli l-ḥamdu li-llāhi wa-salāmun ʿalā ʿibādihi lladhīna ṣṭafā āllāhu khayrun ammā yushrikūna
versets.trad: Say: “Praise be to God, and peace be upon His servants whom He has chosen!”
versets.commentaire: Upon these elect, Qushayrī says magnificently, He bestowed peace from His eternity, while they were yet in the hidden void, within reach of His knowledge and His power, with no existence or act; when He brought them forth into being, He greeted them with that peace, and on the Last Day He will make them hear it. And he adds: He chose them, then guided them, then sheltered them, and greeted them before He created and brought them forth — then, after greeting them with His love, He received them. The greeting precedes the very creation of the beloved.

mots.racine: س ل م / ع ب د

versets.ar: أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَأَنبَتْنَا بِهِ حَدَائِقَ ذَاتَ بَهْجَةٍ مَّا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَن تُنبِتُوا شَجَرَهَا ۗ أَإِلَٰهٌ مَّعَ اللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ
versets.tr: ammāan khalaqa l-samāwāti wa-l-arḍa wa-anzala lakum mina l-samāʾi māʾan fa-anbatnā bihi ḥadāʾiqa dhāta bahjatin mā kāna lakum an tunbitū shajarahā a-ilāhun maʿa llāhi bal hum qawmun yaʿdilūna
versets.trad: Is He not the One who created the heavens and the earth and sent down for you water from the sky, by which We cause to grow gardens full of delight whose trees you could never grow? Is there a god alongside God? No, but they are a people who set up equals.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī interiorizes the gardens: the fruits of the outward are the nourishment of souls, the fruits of the inward and the secrets are the light of hearts; and just as in spring nothing remains of winter's desolation, so there remains in the hearts and the moments of the elect not the least particle of absence, veil, repulsion, or suspicion. The rain from the sky makes the earth green as grace makes the heart green again.

mots.sens: created the heavens and the earth

mots.racine: ح د ق / ب ه ج

versets.ar: أَمَّن جَعَلَ الْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَجَعَلَ خِلَالَهَا أَنْهَارًا وَجَعَلَ لَهَا رَوَاسِيَ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ حَاجِزًا ۗ أَإِلَٰهٌ مَّعَ اللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: ammāan jaʿala l-arḍa qarāran wa-jaʿala khilālahā anhāran wa-jaʿala lahā rawāsiya wa-jaʿala bayna l-baḥrayni ḥājizan a-ilāhun maʿa llāhi bal aktharuhum lā yaʿlamūna
versets.trad: Is He not the One who made the earth a dwelling-place, set rivers through it, placed firm mountains upon it, and made a barrier between the two seas? Is there a god alongside God? No, but most of them do not know!
themes: the barrier of the two seas
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds a series of allegories: the souls of the worshippers are the seat (qarār) of their obedience, the hearts of the knowers the seat of their knowledge, the spirits of the ecstatics the seat of their love, the secrets of the unifiers the seat of their contemplation. And the barrier between the two seas, he says, is set between the heart and the soul, that neither may overcome the other; or between servanthood and the Reality — for if servanthood prevailed, it would be a denial of the Reality, and if the Reality prevailed over servanthood, it would abolish the Law.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ق ر ر
ب ح ر / ح ج ز
mots.sens: a barrier between the two seas

versets.ar: أَمَّن يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذَا دَعَاهُ وَيَكْشِفُ السُّوءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفَاءَ الْأَرْضِ ۗ أَإِلَٰهٌ مَّعَ اللَّهِ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ
versets.tr: ammāan yujību l-muḍṭarra idhā daʿāhu wa-yakshifu l-sūʾa wa-yajʿalukum khulafāʾa l-arḍi a-ilāhun maʿa llāhi qalīlan mā tadhakkarūna
versets.trad: Is He not the One who answers the distressed when he calls upon Him, and removes the evil, and makes you successors on the earth? Is there a god alongside God? Little do you reflect!
versets.commentaire: This is the great verse of the sūra. Qushayrī distinguishes: the answer is by speech, the relief by giving; the response by the Word, the easing by the gift. The call of the distressed (muḍṭarr) has no veil, and likewise the call of the oppressed. But he probes the word: so long as the servant imagines in himself some power, some expedient, some means to lean upon, he is not truly driven to extremity. The truly driven sees himself as the drowning man in the sea, the lost one in the desert: he sees his bridle in the hand of his Master, and knows himself as the dead in the hands of his washer. And he adds: the distressed need not ask another to pray for him, for God has promised to answer the one who supplicates Him, not the one who prays for him.

mots.racine: ج و ب / ض ر ر
ك ش ف / س و أ

versets.ar: أَمَّن يَهْدِيكُمْ فِي ظُلُمَاتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَمَن يُرْسِلُ الرِّيَاحَ بُشْرًا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ ۗ أَإِلَٰهٌ مَّعَ اللَّهِ ۚ تَعَالَى اللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
versets.tr: ammāan yahdīkum fī ẓulumāti l-barri wa-l-baḥri wa-man yursilu l-riyāḥa bushran bayna yaday raḥmatihi a-ilāhun maʿa llāhi taʿālā llāhu ʿammā yushrikūna
versets.trad: Is He not the One who guides you in the darknesses of land and sea, and who sends the winds as glad tidings before His mercy? Is there a god alongside God? Exalted is God above what they associate with Him!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī interiorizes the darknesses into those of the soul: when the moment grows dark in the clash of thoughts, at the hour when the right way becomes uncertain, when the matter closes in upon the anguish of calculation and the darknesses of conjecture — who guides you toward what is right, if not by making you abandon your own management to commit yourself to the decree? And the winds of mercy, he says, are the winds of trust (tawakkul) that purify the hearts of the traces of self-choosing, then make the suns of contentment rise.

mots.racine: ه د ي / ظ ل م
ر س ل / ر و ح / ب ش ر

versets.ar: أَمَّن يَبْدَأُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَمَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۗ أَإِلَٰهٌ مَّعَ اللَّهِ ۚ قُلْ هَاتُوا بُرْهَانَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ
versets.tr: ammāan yabdaʾu l-khalqa thumma yuʿīduhu wa-man yarzuqukum mina l-samāʾi wa-l-arḍi a-ilāhun maʿa llāhi qul hātū burhānakum in kuntum ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: Is He not the One who originates creation, then renews it, and who provides for you from the heaven and the earth? Is there a god alongside God? Say: “Bring your proof, if you are truthful!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī meditates on God's unique mastery over being and sustenance: who brings forth what appears according to His prior decree, who narrows and widens provision, who withholds from some and dispenses to others? Has anyone other than God the power for that? Then the challenge: if you imagine such a thing, show your proof; and since you are unable, why do you not acknowledge the oneness?

mots.racine: ب د أ / خ ل ق

versets.ar: قُل لَّا يَعْلَمُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ ۚ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ
versets.tr: qul lā yaʿlamu man fī l-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi l-ghayba illā llāhu wa-mā yashʿurūna ayyāna yubʿathūna
versets.trad: Say: “None in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except God.” And they do not perceive when they will be resurrected.
versets.commentaire: The Unseen (ghayb), says Qushayrī, is that upon which none can rise, of which creation has no indication, whose knowledge the Real reserves to Himself — and the sciences of creatures fall short of it. Then, when He wills to single out a people with a portion of His knowledge, He alone grants it to them. The Hour of resurrection remains the secret most reserved of all.

mots.racine: ع ل م / غ ي ب

versets.ar: بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِّنْهَا ۖ بَلْ هُم مِّنْهَا عَمُونَ
versets.tr: bali ddāraka ʿilmuhum fī l-ākhirati bal hum fī shakkin minhā bal hum minhā ʿamūna
versets.trad: Nay, does their knowledge attain to the Hereafter? Nay, they are in doubt about it; nay, they are blind to it.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums up their state: they are, in sum, in doubt about it; they neither deny it absolutely nor reject it with certainty. And he generalizes: such is the state of every sick heart — it has no true life nor rest in its despair, being in doubt about the resurrection and holding the second life improbable. Blindness to the Hereafter is the very sickness of the heart.

mots.racine: د ر ك / ع ل م

versets.ar: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَإِذَا كُنَّا تُرَابًا وَآبَاؤُنَا أَئِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qāla lladhīna kafarū a-idhā kunnā turāban wa-ābāʾunā a-innā la-mukhrajūna
versets.trad: Those who disbelieve say: “When we have become dust, we and our fathers, shall we indeed be brought forth?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports their objection: our fathers received this promise before us, without its coming to pass — and we are but like them. Denial is handed down from generation to generation, each measuring the possible by the yardstick of its bounded experience. Inherited doubt stands in for reason.

mots.def: ukhrija: to be brought back to life.

versets.ar: لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا هَٰذَا نَحْنُ وَآبَاؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَٰذَا إِلَّا أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: laqad wuʿidnā hādhā naḥnu wa-ābāʾunā min qablu in hādhā illā asāṭīru l-awwalīna
versets.trad: We have been promised this, we and our fathers before us; these are nothing but the fables of the ancients!”
versets.commentaire: They call the promise “fables of the ancients” (asāṭīr al-awwalīn). Qushayrī notes that this charge is the denier's habitual refuge: what he cannot conceive, he files among tales. But the Real will answer that the term is near — what they take for legend is already at their heels.

mots.racine: س ط ر / أ و ل

versets.ar: قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ
versets.tr: qul sīrū fī l-arḍi fa-nẓurū kayfa kāna ʿāqibatu l-mujrimīna
versets.trad: Say: “Travel through the earth and see how was the end of the criminals.”
versets.commentaire: God invites them to travel the earth to ponder the end of the criminals. Qushayrī, following the verse, makes of the outward journey a metaphor for reflection: the ruins of past peoples are signs for whoever knows how to see. To contemplate the end of the guilty is a warning graven in the very landscape.

mots.racine: ع ق ب / ج ر م

versets.ar: وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُن فِي ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lā taḥzan ʿalayhim wa-lā takun fī ḍayqin mimmā yamkurūna
versets.trad: And do not grieve over them, nor be in distress at what they scheme.
versets.commentaire: God consoles the Prophet: do not grieve at their denial, nor be oppressed by their schemes. Qushayrī, in the line of the verse, sees in it the calming of the heart that commits itself to God: grief and anguish are born of attachment to the outcome, but whoever entrusts the result to God finds peace.

versets.ar: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-yaqūlūna matā hādhā l-waʿdu in kuntum ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: And they say: “When will this promise be, if you are truthful?”
versets.commentaire: They mocked, asking: “When then this promise?” Qushayrī recalls that this mocking question calls forth the divine answer: the term is near. To press the coming of the chastisement in derision is the way of the unbeliever — but the delay is no disproof.

versets.ar: قُلْ عَسَىٰ أَن يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُم بَعْضُ الَّذِي تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ
versets.tr: qul ʿasā an yakūna radifa lakum baʿḍu lladhī tastaʿjilūna
versets.trad: Say: “Perhaps some of what you seek to hasten is already close behind you.”
versets.commentaire: The Real answers, according to Qushayrī: soon their term will fall upon them. “Already close behind you” (radifa) — that is, very near, following close. What the mockers press to see come is nearer to them than they think: derision does not push the Hour away, it draws it near.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-inna rabbaka la-dhū faḍlin ʿalā l-nāsi wa-lākinna aktharahum lā yashkurūna
versets.trad: And truly your Lord is full of bounty toward mankind, but most of them are not thankful.
versets.commentaire: Why this ingratitude? Because, says Qushayrī, they do not distinguish between their trials and their gifts. And rare is the one who knows the difference between what is a blessing from God and what is a trial: so long as the servant falls short of the knowledge of his own good, he may love a thing thinking it good, while his misfortune lies in it; and how many things he takes for a blessing, for which he gives thanks and wishes to keep, are in truth a trial to be endured while imploring God to deliver him from it!

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ
versets.tr: wa-inna rabbaka la-yaʿlamu mā tukinnu ṣudūruhum wa-mā yuʿlinūna
versets.trad: And truly your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they reveal.
versets.commentaire: Nothing of their states is veiled from God, says Qushayrī: the sincere one, whose outward equals his inward, He knows; the hypocrite, whose inward contradicts his outward and who deceives people, He knows; the disbeliever, whose secret and declared are equally in denial, He knows — and He requites each according to His knowledge. How could it be otherwise, since it is He who decreed, judged, and apportioned it?

mots.racine: ك ن ن / ص د ر

versets.ar: وَمَا مِنْ غَائِبَةٍ فِي السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: wa-mā min ghāʾibatin fī l-samāʾi wa-l-arḍi illā fī kitābin mubīnin
versets.trad: There is nothing hidden in the heaven and the earth but it is in a clear Book.
versets.commentaire: Nothing, says Qushayrī, but is recorded in the guarded Tablet: its decree fixed there, His will fulfilled there, His knowledge bound to it. No hidden thing of the heavens and the earth escapes the clear Book. The divine knowledge embraces the absent as the present: nothing is lost, nothing forgotten.

mots.racine: س م و / أ ر ض
mots.sens: in the heaven and the earth

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ب ي ن

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَقُصُّ عَلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ أَكْثَرَ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ
versets.tr: inna hādhā l-qurʾāna yaquṣṣu ʿalā banī isrāʾīla akthara lladhī hum fīhi yakhtalifūna
versets.trad: Indeed this Qurʾān relates to the Children of Israel most of that over which they differ.
versets.commentaire: The Qurʾān settles the divergences of the Children of Israel. Qushayrī, following the verse, sees in it a grace of the revealed Word: where men tear at one another in interpretations, the clear Book restores the truth. The Qurʾān is not only narrative but arbitration of what divides.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّهُ لَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: wa-innahu la-hudan wa-raḥmatun li-l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: And it is indeed a guidance and a mercy for the believers.
themes: the grace of the preserved Book
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī underscores here the privilege of this community: God has guarded their Book and preserved from all alteration that by which they order their religion — an immense blessing for which few give thanks. The Qurʾān is guidance and mercy, unlike their Book which the Truthful One declared falsified: the Word kept intact is itself a mercy.

versets.ar: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُم بِحُكْمِهِ ۚ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ
versets.tr: inna rabbaka yaqḍī baynahum bi-ḥukmihi wa-huwa l-ʿazīzu l-ʿalīmu
versets.trad: Indeed your Lord will judge between them by His decree. He is the Mighty, the All-Knowing.
versets.commentaire: God will decide between them by His decree. Qushayrī glosses the two Names: “the Mighty” who gives might to the believers, “the All-Knowing” who knows what each deserves — the immense reward or the painful chastisement. The divine judgment joins sovereignty to perfect knowledge of hearts.

mots.racine: ع ز ز / ع ل م

versets.ar: فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَى الْحَقِّ الْمُبِينِ
versets.tr: fa-tawakkal ʿalā llāhi innaka ʿalā l-ḥaqqi l-mubīni
versets.trad: So put your trust in God; you are upon the manifest truth.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī develops the command of tawakkul: apply yourself to discharging what is prescribed for you, and rely on the truth of His promise in your help and your sustenance. Let not what befalls you from them as harm dismay you: all of it occurs only by Our permission if it is an evil, and by Our disposing if it is a good. You are upon the truth and the clarity of the real, and they upon doubt and the darkness of associationism.

versets.ar: إِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتَىٰ وَلَا تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ الدُّعَاءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ
versets.tr: innaka lā tusmiʿu l-mawtā wa-lā tusmiʿu l-ṣumma l-duʿāʾa idhā wallaw mudbirīna
versets.trad: You cannot make the dead hear, nor make the deaf hear the call when they turn away in flight.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī interprets: these are those whose hearts God has made dead through associationism and whom He has made deaf to the hearing of the truth. It is not in the Prophet's power to guide them or wrench them from doubt. The deafness in question is not of the ear but of the heart turned away.

mots.racine: س م ع / م و ت
mots.sens: you cannot make the dead hear

mots.racine: ص م م / د ع و
و ل ي / د ب ر
mots.def: wallā mudbiran: to turn one's back.

versets.ar: وَمَا أَنتَ بِهَادِي الْعُمْيِ عَن ضَلَالَتِهِمْ ۖ إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِآيَاتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā anta bi-hādī l-ʿumyi ʿan ḍalālatihim in tusmiʿu illā man yuʾminu bi-āyātinā fa-hum muslimūna
versets.trad: Nor can you guide the blind out of their error. You make none hear but those who believe in Our signs and are submitters.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī specifies the nuance: you guide them by the call and the indication, but you guide no one in the sense of removing the false from the heart and inclining it toward knowledge — for removing and inclining are not in your power. You make none hear but the one whom We have made happy through grace and orientation to the way. The guidance that transforms belongs to God alone; to the messenger belongs the call.

mots.racine: ه د ي / ع م ي
أ م ن / أ ي ي

versets.ar: وَإِذَا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنَا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِّنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ النَّاسَ كَانُوا بِآيَاتِنَا لَا يُوقِنُونَ
versets.tr: wa-idhā waqaʿa l-qawlu ʿalayhim akhrajnā lahum dābbatan mina l-arḍi tukallimuhum anna l-nāsa kānū bi-āyātinā lā yūqinūna
versets.trad: And when the Word falls upon them, We shall bring forth for them a beast from the earth that will speak to them, that mankind had no certainty in Our signs.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports this eschatological sign: when the promise of resurrection is fulfilled, We shall make plain its portents through the speech of the Beast brought forth from the earth, and through other signs. The coming forth of the Beast (dābba) marks the threshold where faith is no longer received: a sign that the time of trial is ending.

mots.racine: و ق ع / ق و ل
د ب ب / أ ر ض

versets.ar: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجًا مِّمَّن يُكَذِّبُ بِآيَاتِنَا فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-yawma naḥshuru min kulli ummatin fawjan mimman yukadhdhibu bi-āyātinā fa-hum yūzaʿūna
versets.trad: And on the Day We shall gather from every nation a throng of those who denied Our signs, and they shall be marshaled.
versets.commentaire: From every community a throng of those who denied the signs will be gathered. Qushayrī notes that at that moment, neither faith nor excuse profits any longer. The Day of gathering ranks the deniers: the time of repentance is past, that of requital begins.

versets.ar: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءُوا قَالَ أَكَذَّبْتُم بِآيَاتِي وَلَمْ تُحِيطُوا بِهَا عِلْمًا أَمَّاذَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: ḥattā idhā jāʾū qāla a-kadhdhabtum bi-āyātī wa-lam tuḥīṭū bihā ʿilman ammādhā kuntum taʿmalūna
versets.trad: Until, when they come, He will say: “Did you deny My signs without encompassing them in knowledge? Or what were you doing?”
versets.commentaire: God confronts them: they denied the signs without grasping them in knowledge. Qushayrī, following the verse, stresses the absurdity of their denial: to reject without knowing. Their fault is not the error of the ignorant who seeks, but the hasty rejection of one who refuses to look. The divine question lays bare the void of their denial.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / أ ي ي
ح و ط / ع ل م
mots.def: aḥāṭa ʿilman: to grasp by knowledge.

versets.ar: وَوَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِم بِمَا ظَلَمُوا فَهُمْ لَا يَنطِقُونَ
versets.tr: wa-waqaʿa l-qawlu ʿalayhim bi-mā ẓalamū fa-hum lā yanṭiqūna
versets.trad: And the Word will fall upon them because of their wrongdoing, and they will not speak.
versets.commentaire: The verdict falls upon them because of their injustice, and they remain mute. Qushayrī, in the line of the verse, contrasts this muteness with their former disputings: before the evidence of the decree, not a word more. The injustice that spoke loudly in this world falls silent on the Day of truth.

mots.racine: و ق ع / ق و ل

versets.ar: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِيَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَالنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: a-lam yaraw annā jaʿalnā l-layla li-yaskunū fīhi wa-l-nahāra mubṣiran inna fī dhālika la-āyātin li-qawmin yuʾminūna
versets.trad: Have they not seen that We made the night for them to rest in it, and the day giving light? In that are signs for a people who believe.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls the sense of the cosmic rhythm: the night was made for the time of their rest, and the day for the time of seeking their livelihood. The regular alternation of rest and activity is a sign for whoever believes: creation itself is ordered to the repose and the labor of man.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ل ي ل / س ك ن
mots.sens: We made the night for rest

mots.racine: ن ه ر / ب ص ر
أ ي ي / أ م ن

versets.ar: وَيَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَن شَاءَ اللَّهُ ۚ وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ دَاخِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-yawma yunfakhu fī l-ṣūri fa-faziʿa man fī l-samāwāti wa-man fī l-arḍi illā man shāʾa llāhu wa-kullun atawhu dākhirīna
versets.trad: And on the Day the Trumpet is blown, all who are in the heavens and on the earth will be terrified, save whom God wills, and all will come to Him humbled.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī meditates on this Day when the souls are torn from the bodies: one soul rises to the Heights (ʿilliyyūn), another descends to the Pit (sijjīn). The first dwell in the crops of birds that graze in Paradise and take refuge by night in lamps suspended beneath the Throne — their state is glorification, spirit, and rest; and to some is given contemplation and vision, according to the merit of what they were here below. All come to Him in humility.

mots.racine: ن ف خ / ص و ر

mots.def: faziʿa: to be struck with terror.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / د خ ر

versets.ar: وَتَرَى الْجِبَالَ تَحْسَبُهَا جَامِدَةً وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحَابِ ۚ صُنْعَ اللَّهِ الَّذِي أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ ۚ إِنَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَفْعَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-tarā l-jibāla taḥsabuhā jāmidatan wa-hiya tamurru marra l-saḥābi ṣunʿa llāhi lladhī atqana kulla shayʾin innahu khabīrun bi-mā tafʿalūna
versets.trad: And you will see the mountains, which you supposed firm, passing by like the passing of clouds. This is the work of God who has perfected all things. He is indeed aware of what you do.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī applies this verse to the masters of stability (tamkīn): many men today are still in their souls yet travel in the Kingdom through their secrets; the allusion is to them. As is said of the knower: he is present-absent — present to people through his outward, absent from all creation through his secrets. The mountains one supposes fixed pass by like clouds: the apparent calm may overlie the swiftest inner journey.

mots.racine: ح س ب / ج م د
م ر ر / س ح ب

versets.ar: مَن جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا وَهُم مِّن فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ
versets.tr: man jāʾa bi-l-ḥasanati fa-lahu khayrun minhā wa-hum min fazaʿin yawmaʾidhin āminūna
versets.trad: Whoever comes with a good deed shall have better than it, and they will be secure from terror that Day.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī probes the word “better” (khayr): it may be an emphasis — what he will have as reward in the Hereafter is better than nearness itself; or: he will have a better portion, a better outcome, a better reward than his deed. And they are secure from the terror of the Day. The good deed is repaid a hundredfold, and the universal terror passes them by.

mots.racine: ج ي أ / ح س ن
ف ز ع / أ م ن

versets.ar: وَمَن جَاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَكُبَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِي النَّارِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-man jāʾa bi-l-sayyiʾati fa-kubbat wujūhuhum fī l-nāri hal tujzawna illā mā kuntum taʿmalūna
versets.trad: And whoever comes with an evil deed — their faces will be cast into the Fire: “Are you requited for anything but what you used to do?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sets up the symmetry: just as the state of the obedient is today the opposite of theirs, their lot tomorrow in the Hereafter will be the reverse. Those who come with evil are cast face-first into the Fire — requited for their deeds alone. Divine justice only renders to each what he himself has produced.

mots.racine: ج ي أ / س و أ
ك ب ب / و ج ه

versets.ar: إِنَّمَا أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ رَبَّ هَٰذِهِ الْبَلْدَةِ الَّذِي حَرَّمَهَا وَلَهُ كُلُّ شَيْءٍ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ
versets.tr: innamā umirtu an aʿbuda rabba hādhihi l-baldati lladhī ḥarramahā wa-lahu kullu shayʾin wa-umirtu an akūna mina l-muslimīna
versets.trad: I have only been commanded to worship the Lord of this town, who has made it sacred, and to whom belongs all things; and I have been commanded to be of those who submit,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums up the mission: He has commanded him the pure religion (ḥanīfiyya), the disavowal of associationism, both manifest and hidden, and adherence to the straight way. To worship the Lord of this sanctified town — Mecca — and to be among the submitters: such is the essential commandment, stripped of all shirk.

mots.racine: أ م ر / ع ب د
ر ب ب / ب ل د

versets.ar: وَأَنْ أَتْلُوَ الْقُرْآنَ ۖ فَمَنِ اهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُنذِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-an atluwa l-qurʾāna fa-mani htadā fa-innamā yahtadī li-nafsihi wa-man ḍalla fa-qul innamā anā mina l-mundhirīna
versets.trad: and to recite the Qurʾān.” So whoever is guided is guided only for himself; and as for him who strays, say: “I am only one of the warners.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī adds: He has announced that whoever follows him and believes him, the Real makes him His protégé and establishes his right. To recite the Qurʾān is to bear guidance; whoever is guided is guided for himself, whoever strays strays against himself. The Prophet's role is not to compel but to warn — guidance remains in God's hands.

mots.racine: ت ل و / ق ر أ

versets.ar: وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ سَيُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ فَتَعْرِفُونَهَا ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: wa-quli l-ḥamdu li-llāhi sa-yurīkum āyātihi fa-taʿrifūnahā wa-mā rabbuka bi-ghāfilin ʿammā taʿmalūna
versets.trad: And say: “Praise be to God! He will show you His signs, and you will recognize them.” And your Lord is not unaware of what you do.
versets.commentaire: The seal of the sūra, which Qushayrī comments with a poignant stroke: He will soon show you His signs — happy is the one who returns before his death, and woe to the one who returns only after the time has gone and the moment is lost! Praise opens and closes the sūra; between the two, the pressing call to return while there is yet time.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / أ ي ي
mots.sens: He will show you His signs
